eras.name: The Age of Discoveries
eras.description: Portugal's maritime empire introduced exotic silks, spices, and gemstones from Asia and Africa, profoundly shaping court dress. The Manueline aesthetic—ornate and sea-inspired—translated into fashion through rich brocades and imported textiles.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 16th-century Portuguese navigator standing on a caravel deck, wearing an ornate crimson silk doublet with gold thread embroidery, a dark velvet capa over one shoulder, cinematic ocean horizon and rope rigging.

eras.name: Baroque under Iberian Union
eras.description: Under Spanish Habsburg rule, Portuguese court fashion adopted the rigid black silhouettes of Spanish dress while maintaining distinct local embroidery traditions. Lace and gold filigree became markers of Portuguese identity.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 17th-century Portuguese noblewoman in Lisbon wearing a stiff black velvet gown with white lace ruff collar, gold filigree earrings and necklace, standing in an azulejo-tiled hall with candlelight.

eras.name: Pombaline Reconstruction
eras.description: After the 1755 earthquake, Portugal rebuilt Lisbon with Enlightenment rationality. Fashion reflected this modernization with cleaner silhouettes influenced by French tastes, while rural folk dress preserved deep regional traditions.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A late 18th-century Portuguese couple in rebuilt Pombaline Lisbon: the man in a French-influenced blue riding coat with brass buttons, the woman in an embroidered regional Minho bodice with gold filigree heart pendant.

eras.name: Estado Novo National Identity
eras.description: The Salazar regime promoted idealized folk costumes as symbols of national identity. Regional dress from the Minho, Alentejo, and Azores was codified and displayed at state-sponsored festivals while urban elites followed Parisian fashion.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1950s Portuguese folk festival scene: women in colorful Minho costumes with layered gold necklaces and embroidered aprons, dancing in a village square surrounded by whitewashed buildings.

eras.name: Post-Revolution Freedom
eras.description: The 1974 Carnation Revolution liberated Portuguese fashion from conservative norms. Youth embraced denim, leather, and global counterculture aesthetics while a new generation of designers began emerging in Lisbon and Porto.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Young Portuguese revolutionaries in 1974 Lisbon, wearing olive military jackets with red carnations pinned to lapels, flared jeans and leather boots, walking through a sun-drenched cobblestone street.

eras.name: The New Portuguese Wave
eras.description: A renaissance in Portuguese design driven by sustainability, cork innovation, and a new generation of designers gaining international recognition. Lisbon and Porto have become creative hubs blending artisanal heritage with contemporary minimalism.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A contemporary Portuguese fashion editorial on a Lisbon rooftop overlooking the Tagus River, model wearing a minimalist cream linen suit with cork leather bag and sandals, golden hour light.

textiles.name: Burel
textiles.description: A dense, water-resistant wool felt traditionally used by shepherds in the Serra da Estrela mountains, now reimagined for contemporary fashion and design.
textiles.technique: Wool is woven then fulled through washing and beating to create a dense, weatherproof felt

textiles.name: Arraiolos Tapestry
textiles.description: Cross-stitch wool embroidery on linen canvas, featuring Moorish-inspired geometric and floral designs dating back to the 13th century.
textiles.technique: Hand-stitched cross-stitch embroidery on jute or linen canvas

textiles.name: Cork Fabric
textiles.description: Portugal produces over half the world's cork, and its thin bark sheets are laminated to fabric backing to create a sustainable, vegan leather alternative.
textiles.technique: Thin cork bark is shaved, laminated onto cotton or polyester backing, then treated for flexibility

textiles.name: Linen of Guimarães
textiles.description: Fine handwoven linen with a centuries-old tradition, historically used for elaborate trousseau and ecclesiastical vestments.
textiles.technique: Traditional shuttle loom weaving with hand-spun flax fiber

textiles.name: Rendas de Bilros (Bobbin Lace)
textiles.description: Delicate bobbin lace with maritime-inspired motifs, traditionally made by fishermen's wives while waiting for boats to return.
textiles.technique: Pillow lace worked with bobbins on a cylindrical cushion using intricate pin-placement patterns

climate.fashionImplication: Mild winters and warm summers favor layered linen and cotton ensembles. The Atlantic coast demands weather-resistant outerwear, while the southern Algarve encourages lightweight resort wear year-round.

culturalInfluences: Maritime exploration and global trade routes
Azulejo tile art and decorative traditions
Fado music and the concept of 'saudade'
Rural folk traditions of Minho, Alentejo, and Azores

notableEvents: Sangue Novo (New Blood) emerging designer competition
